html:
Antony Waste Handling Cell: Mispriced From Contractor To Waste Infrastructure Platform

Antony Waste is no longer just a municipal collection contractor. It is becoming a scarce listed Indian waste infrastructure platform with collection, processing, RDF, compost, EPR credits, and waste-to-energy assets. The stock closed at ₹471.40 on May 15, 2026, down about 32% from its 52-week high, with a market cap near ₹1,366 crore. That weakness matters because the business has improved while retail attention has faded.

The market is focused on the messy pieces: Q3 FY26 EBITDA margin fell to 18.4%, municipal receivables remain lumpy, and the next waste-to-energy capex cycle will raise debt. That is the wrong anchor. Q3 was distorted by an 82-day PCMC WtE shutdown for technical modifications, higher employee costs, and new manpower additions. The plant was back online from December 18, and management said normalized PCMC generation at 85% PLF is roughly 9.25 million units per quarter, or ₹13-14 crore of quarterly revenue. Meanwhile, Q4 FY26 operating data already showed the real direction: total MSW handled rose 23% in Q4 and 15% for FY26, processing tonnage rose 32% in Q4 and 19% for FY26, RDF sales hit a record 177,000 tonnes, and core revenue grew 13%.

The variant perception is that AWHCL is being valued on FY26 noise just as the order book quality is improving. The AG Enviro merger, effective December 31, 2025, removes a structural holding-company friction and gives AWHCL cleaner access to subsidiary cash flows. The two BMC C&T contracts add roughly ₹1,330 crore of seven-year revenue potential, consolidated into Antony’s books with minority interest below the line. The Thane pre-processing DBOT project adds a 600-800 TPD facility, a ₹330 crore ten-year contract value, and about ₹18-20 crore annual O&M revenue once commissioned around December 2026, with the ₹67 crore project capex reimbursable by TMC.

The bigger torque is Andhra Pradesh. Antony Lara has partnered with JFE Engineering for two 15 MW WtE plants in Kadapa and Kurnool, with JFE taking 25% stakes in the project SPVs. JFE’s own announcement says each plant will accept up to 1,000 TPD of MSW, convert about 750 TPD into green energy, and target completion and commercial operation in 2028. Antony’s Q4 update says the PPA tariff is ₹8.10/unit, expected commercial operations are Q1 FY29, and estimated revenue over the 20-year concession is about ₹3,200 crore. This is not a promotional small-cap JV. It brings a global WtE operator with 250-plus facilities into the cap table and EPC stack, reducing execution risk where the Indian market historically gives little credit.

At today’s price, the stock is around 7.5x estimated FY26 EBITDA using ₹225-230 crore of FY26 EBITDA, Q3 net debt of about ₹350 crore, and current market cap. That multiple is not expensive for a recurring municipal infrastructure business, and it is plainly cheap if EBITDA is moving toward ₹325-375 crore before the Andhra projects fully mature. The market is also not paying much for EPR monetization. PCMC WtE has already monetized 20% of its first-year allocation of more than 94,400 tonnes of EPR credits, which could become a small but high-margin side stream if the regulatory market deepens.

The bear case is easy to describe: WtE plants have downtime, municipalities can delay payments, C&T contracts are labor and fuel heavy, and leverage will rise as Andhra construction begins. Management itself expects net debt/equity to move from 0.4x toward 1.0-1.2x over the next couple of years. That is real risk. But the risk is largely financing and execution timing, not product-market fit. India generates over 150,000 TPD of municipal solid waste, organized processing penetration remains low, and municipalities increasingly need private operators with technical credentials. In WtE specifically, management said Andhra saw only two bids, which tells you the competitive field is narrow.

The base case is that AWHCL compounds core revenue in the mid-to-high teens through FY28 from BMC, Thane, processing growth, tariff escalation, RDF, and normalized PCMC output, then steps up again in FY29 as Andhra begins contributing. Normalized FY29 consolidated EBITDA of ₹360-380 crore is plausible without heroic assumptions. At 8.5x EV/EBITDA, less higher project debt and minority value, the equity is worth about ₹780 per share.

Bear case: ₹330 target, 30% downside. This assumes PCMC remains inconsistent, Andhra consumes capital without a valuation uplift, EBITDA stagnates near ₹235 crore, and the market applies 6.5x EBITDA.

Base case: ₹780 target, 65% upside. This assumes FY29 EBITDA around ₹370 crore, successful BMC and Thane ramp-up, Andhra on schedule, and an 8.5x infrastructure multiple.

Bull case: ₹1,240 target, 163% upside. This assumes Andhra commissions cleanly, another WtE win emerges, EBITDA approaches ₹470 crore, and the stock rerates to 10x as investors stop treating Antony as a collection contractor and start valuing it as India’s listed waste infrastructure proxy.
